- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
==== ISSUE 148 ====
TITLE: Inconsistent @runtime_checkable usage: seam Protocols give name-only validation; adapter contract Protocols give none
SEV: LOW
FILE: src/assay_engine/contracts/parser.py:16; src/assay_engine/contracts/claims.py:42; src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:33; src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:43; src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py:24; src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:172
LINE: 
DESC: The seam Protocols (DataVersioner, Tracer, ExperimentTracker, VectorStore, the reasoning seam) are decorated @runtime_checkable, but the core engine↔adapter contract Protocols a clone must implement — IngestionParser, ExternalClaimsSource, FeatureBuilder, BaselineBuilder, TimestampAuthority, HeldOutConfirmer, ClaimConfirmer — are NOT. This is a stability inconsistency with two distinct hazards: (1) isinstance against the adapter Protocols raises TypeError ('Instance and class checks can only be used with @runtime_checkable protocols'), so neither the engine nor a clone can defensively isinstance-check an adapter object — PoC verified. (2) The @runtime_checkable that DOES exist is a false-confidence trap: it validates method NAMES only, not signatures, so isinstance(WrongVersioner(), DataVersioner) returns True even when put()/fingerprint() take the wrong arguments (PoC verified). Meanwhile none of the @runtime_checkable seam Protocols are actually isinstance-checked anywhere in src/, so the decorator is currently dead weight that invites callers (and clones) to rely on a check that wouldn't catch a real misimplementation.
FIX: Pick one consistent policy. Recommended: keep adapter validation behavior-based (call the methods, normalize failures to typed errors as the parser/Verdict fixes above do) and DROP the @runtime_checkable decorators that are never used, OR document explicitly that runtime_checkable is name-only and is not a substitute for signature/behavior validation. If isinstance-based adapter validation is desired, make the contract Protocols runtime_checkable too — but pair it with explicit call-time checks since names-only isinstance will not catch wrong signatures/return types.

==== ISSUE 149 ====
TITLE: FeatureMatrix accepts non-numeric rows despite its numeric type contract
SEV: LOW
FILE: src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:17-30
LINE: 
DESC: FeatureMatrix declares rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...] and the module docstring states output is 'a plain numeric FeatureMatrix ... so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.' __post_init__ validates row COUNT and WIDTH but never that the cells are numeric. PoC verified: FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),)) is accepted. A FeatureBuilder adapter that emits strings/None therefore passes construction and run_study's ingest-time checks (which only validate unit_ids dedup/ghost ids, pipeline.py:330-340), letting non-numeric 'features' flow into any downstream numeric consumer and fail far from the source — a silent break on a minor adapter change, with the only contract signal being a type annotation the runtime ignores.
FIX: Validate cell types in FeatureMatrix.__post_init__: reject any cell that is not a real, finite number (e.g. `if not isinstance(x, (int, float)) or isinstance(x, bool) or not math.isfinite(x): raise ValueError(...)`), mirroring the finite-float discipline already enforced in confirm._validate_finite and the canonical hashing path.

verdicts.notes: Read src/assay_engine/methodology/verdict.py:39-52: __post_init__ now raises TypeError if label is not a VerdictLabel and ValueError if hypothesis_id is not a non-empty str, then freezes evidence. This converts the opaque KeyError in _score / AttributeError on verdict.label.value into a fail-fast, attributable contract error at construction — exactly the finding's defect. Regression test tests/test_contract.py:118 test_verdict_rejects_non_enum_label asserts pytest.raises(TypeError, match='VerdictLabel') for Verdict('h1', label='supported', ...) plus the empty-hypothesis_id case and a happy path. Pure type guard, dataset-agnostic, no ADR-0002/0003 impact. tests/test_pipeline.py+test_contract.py: 58 passed after restore.

Read src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:357-362 (INGEST phase): after the try/except that normalizes parser failures to IngestionError, the code now does `if not isinstance(corpus, Corpus): raise IngestionError('parser returned ... expected Corpus')` and similarly guards source_fp is a str, before `corpus.units` (line 363). This honors the documented 'Raises IngestionError on a bad source' contract for a parser returning None/list/dict, replacing the opaque AttributeError. Regression test tests/test_pipeline.py:124 test_parser_returning_non_corpus_raises_ingestion_error drives a _NoneParser through run_study and asserts pytest.raises(IngestionError, match='expected Corpus').
